shortcut: Pronouns replace nouns. Dutch has six kinds you'll use daily: subject form (ik / jij), object form (mij / jou), possessive (mijn / jouw), demonstrative (dit / dat / deze / die), reflexive (me / je / zich), and formal (u).
explanation: Pronouns are the small words that stand in for nouns you've already mentioned — 'Jan is happy. He smiles.' Dutch has the same categories as English, but with one extra complication: pronouns change form depending on what JOB they're doing in the sentence (subject? object? possessive?). This rule gives you the full tables for each type, with English equivalents alongside.

patterns.rule: 1. Personal pronouns — SUBJECT form (doing the action)
examples: Used when the pronoun is the SUBJECT of the sentence (the doer of the verb).

examples.caption: Singular — I / you / he / she / it

examples: ↳ 'jij' / 'wij' / 'zij' are the FULL forms (stressed). 'je' / 'we' / 'ze' are the REDUCED forms (unstressed) — more common in everyday speech.
⚠ 'zij/ze' is both 'she' AND 'they' — context disambiguates. The verb agreement tells you which: 'zij IS' = she is, 'zij ZIJN' = they are.

patterns.rule: 2. Personal pronouns — OBJECT form (receiving the action)
examples: Used when the pronoun is the OBJECT — receives the action or comes after a preposition. Different forms from the subject pronouns above.
↳ 'hen' = direct object 'them' (Ik zie hen). 'hun' = indirect object 'to them' (Ik geef hun het boek). In casual Belgian speech this distinction is often dropped — 'ze' covers both.

examples.caption: Same sentence with subject vs object pronoun visible

examples: Used to show OWNERSHIP. Always sits BEFORE the noun they modify, like an adjective.
↳ 'ons' goes with het-words, 'onze' goes with de-words: 'ons huis' (our house, het) vs 'onze tafel' (our table, de).
⚠ 'haar' and 'zijn' look the same as 'her' and 'his/its' in English — but Dutch 'zijn' is ALSO the infinitive of 'to be'. Context tells you which.

examples.caption: Possessive pronoun in a full sentence

patterns.rule: 4. Demonstrative pronouns — this / that / these / those
examples: Used to POINT — to a specific thing nearby (this/these) or further away (that/those). Dutch picks the form based on de/het AND singular/plural.

examples.caption: Demonstratives — near vs far × de/het × singular/plural

examples: Examples in action:
deze auto (this car) — auto is a de-word, near
dit huis (this house) — huis is a het-word, near
deze huizen (these houses) — plural, all 'deze'
die auto (that car) — far, de-word
dat huis (that house) — far, het-word
die huizen (those houses) — far, plural
↳ Mnemonic: 'd-T' words (dit, dat) go with 'h-T' words (het). 'd-E' words (deze, die) go with 'd-E' words (de) and all plurals.

patterns.rule: 5. Reflexive pronouns — when the action is on yourself
examples: Used with reflexive verbs (zich wassen, zich vergissen, zich herinneren). The pronoun matches the subject and means '-self' / 'myself' / 'himself'.

examples.caption: Same with 'hij' — note the form change to 'zich'

examples: ↳ Only 3rd person (hij/zij/het, zij plural) and formal 'u' use 'zich'. Everyone else uses an object-pronoun-like form (me / je / ons / je).

patterns.rule: 6. Formal U — when to use it and what it covers
examples: 'U' is the formal 'you' — used with strangers, elders, authority figures, in customer-service settings, and in writing. Belgian Dutch leans on it more than NL Dutch.
'U' covers both subject AND object roles: 'U bent welkom' (you are welcome) / 'Ik help u' (I help you).
Possessive is 'uw': 'Uw boek' (your book) / 'Uw paspoort, alstublieft' (your passport, please).
Reflexive with u is 'zich': 'Wast u zich?' (Are you washing yourself?).
↳ When in doubt in Belgium, use 'u' with anyone you don't know well. Switching to 'jij' should be invited by the other person.
'ze' (unstressed) vs 'zij' (stressed, used for emphasis): 'Ze komt vandaag' (she's coming today) vs 'ZIJ komt vandaag, niet hij' (SHE's coming today, not him).
'haar' has two meanings: 'her' (object pronoun + possessive). Context separates them: 'Ik zie haar' (I see her — object) vs 'haar boek' (her book — possessive).
'je' is the busiest pronoun in Dutch: subject (jij), object (jou), possessive (jouw), reflexive (je/jou) — ALL can reduce to 'je'. Verb form usually disambiguates.

examples.caption: 'je' wearing four hats in four sentences

examples: ↳ Don't overthink 'je' — listen for the verb form and sentence shape, and the role is obvious.

memory: 🧠 Six pronoun types: SUBJECT (ik, jij, hij…), OBJECT (mij, jou, hem…), POSSESSIVE (mijn, jouw, zijn…), DEMONSTRATIVE (dit/dat/deze/die — split by de/het/plural), REFLEXIVE (me/je/zich…), and FORMAL 'u' (covers subject + object). 'jij/je', 'wij/we', 'zij/ze' are stressed/unstressed pairs of the same pronoun.

examples.caption: Subject pronoun → object pronoun in one sentence

practice.nl: Wat is het verschil tussen 'ik' en 'mij'?
'Ik' is subject — de doener van de actie. 'Mij' is object — degene die de actie ondergaat. 'Ik zie hem' (subject) vs 'Hij ziet mij' (object).
practice.en: 'Ik' is subject — the doer of the action. 'Mij' is object — the receiver. 'Ik zie hem' vs 'Hij ziet mij'.

practice.nl: 'Huis' is een het-woord, dus 'ons huis' (niet 'onze huis'). 'Onze' gaat met de-woorden: 'onze tafel'.
practice.en: 'Huis' is a het-word, so 'ons huis' (not 'onze huis'). 'Onze' goes with de-words: 'onze tafel'.
When do you use 'die' and when 'dat'?

practice.nl: 'Die' bij de-woorden en bij meervoud: 'die auto', 'die boeken'. 'Dat' bij het-woorden enkelvoud: 'dat huis'. Beide betekenen 'that/those'.
practice.en: 'Die' with de-words and plurals. 'Dat' with het-word singulars. Both mean 'that/those'.

practice.nl: 'Ik was me 's morgens.' — 'me' is het reflexief pronomen voor 'ik'.
practice.en: 'Ik was me 's morgens.' — 'me' is the reflexive pronoun for 'ik'.

practice.nl: Met wie gebruik je 'u' in België?
practice.en: Whom do you use 'u' with in Belgium?

practice.nl: Met onbekenden, ouderen, klanten, leerkrachten — iedereen die je niet goed kent. Beter te formeel dan te informeel.
practice.en: With strangers, elders, customers, teachers — anyone you don't know well. Better too formal than too informal.
